title: Trapped Inventory Release
focus_questions: Which side is actually trapped, and where did that become true?
Did the trapped side get a reclaim chance and still fail?

summary.thesis: A trapped-inventory release occurs when one side loses acceptance beyond its failure point and then fuels the move in the opposite direction by being forced out.
summary.operator_translation: Do not call inventory trapped until the market accepts beyond the failure point and the failed side cannot recover.
summary.ai_usage_note: Use this card when failure, failed reclaim, and acceleration away from the old level appear together.
summary.ai_brief: Environment: one side previously committed near a meaningful level and is now losing control. Required evidence: acceptance beyond the failure point, failed reclaim, and opposite-side acceleration. Do not trade: if the market is still rotating around the level or the failed side can still recover it. Real trigger: failed reclaim after acceptance beyond the failure point. Failure point: immediate recovery and hold back through the failed level.

when_relevant: A prior defending or breakout side is now under pressure.
Price accepts beyond the level that side needed to hold.
The failed side gets a reclaim attempt and still cannot recover.
required_evidence: initiative_drive or equivalent pressure moves beyond the failure point
price shows acceptance beyond the failed level instead of only a wick-through
the failed side cannot recover the level on the next meaningful retest
confirmation_signals: price accepts beyond the original defended or breakout level
the trapped side gets a reclaim chance and still fails
the opposite side accelerates after the failed reclaim
invalidation_signals: the failed side immediately reclaims and holds the level again
price never stabilizes beyond the failure point
the opposite side cannot accelerate after the failed reclaim
no_trade_conditions: Do not call inventory trapped before price has actually accepted beyond the failed level.
Do not chase the release if the market is still rotating around the failed level with no clear acceptance.
Do not assume trapped inventory if the side that supposedly failed can immediately reclaim and hold its level again.

entry_archetypes.name: failed reclaim release
entry_archetypes.description: Enter with the opposite side after the trapped side fails its first meaningful reclaim.
entry_archetypes.risk_note: Avoid if reclaim failure still happens inside unresolved chop.

entry_archetypes.name: acceptance beyond failure point
entry_archetypes.description: Enter after the market proves acceptance beyond the point where the trapped side had to hold.
entry_archetypes.risk_note: Avoid if acceptance is only a brief overshoot.

management_notes: Separate discomfort from true trapped inventory.
The best release often comes from the failed reclaim, not the first break itself.
Once the forced exit has already traveled far, late chasing often hands off into balance.
review_questions: Where exactly did the original side become wrong, not just uncomfortable?
Did the market truly accept beyond the failure point, or only test it briefly?
Was the trade taken on real trapped inventory release, or just on a fast move without inventory evidence?
